title: The Surprising History of Sneakers
summary: A narrator traces how athletic shoes evolved from rubber-soled sandals to global fashion icons, examining the social forces that turned a practical item into a cultural symbol.
body: Few items in a modern wardrobe have had a stranger journey than the sneaker. What started as a practical solution to a very specific problem has become one of the most recognisable symbols of popular culture around the world. The story begins in the mid-nineteenth century, when rubber-soled shoes were first developed for croquet and tennis players who needed quiet, grippy footwear on grass courts. The name 'sneaker' was actually coined because the soft rubber soles allowed wearers to sneak up on people without making any noise. Early models were heavy and uncomfortable by today's standards, but they represented a radical departure from the leather boots that most working people wore every day. By the early twentieth century, canvas shoes with rubber soles had been adopted by basketball players in the United States. The Converse All Star, introduced in 1917, was originally designed as a serious performance shoe. It was endorsed by the player Chuck Taylor, whose name has been printed on the ankle patch ever since. For decades, sports shoes were seen as purely functional objects that belonged on courts and tracks, not on city streets. That began to change in the 1970s, when jogging became fashionable as a leisure activity and ordinary people started wearing trainers outside the gym. Companies like Nike and Adidas, which had previously focused on professional athletes, realised they could sell directly to a mass market. The introduction of the Nike Air cushioning technology in 1979 was a turning point, because it moved athletic shoes into the territory of visible, desirable technology. By the 1980s, hip-hop culture had transformed the sneaker into a statement of identity. Groups like Run-DMC famously wore Adidas shell-toes without laces, and Adidas responded by offering them an endorsement deal worth one million dollars — reportedly the first endorsement deal of its kind with a non-athlete act. The Air Jordan line, launched in collaboration with Michael Jordan in 1985, created what many analysts have described as the modern sneaker market. Shoes that had been made to be worn were increasingly being collected, displayed, and traded like art. Today, rare sneakers can sell at auction for hundreds of thousands of dollars. Sneaker culture has been criticised for encouraging consumerism and for the exploitative labour conditions sometimes found in the factories that produce these shoes. But it has also been praised for democratising fashion — giving people from all economic backgrounds a way to express taste, identity, and belonging through an object that does not require a suit or a designer handbag to signal status.

annotations.exampleEn: Rubber-soled shoes were first developed for tennis players.
The name 'sneaker' was coined because of the quiet rubber soles.
The soft soles allowed wearers to sneak up on people.

grammarNotes.title: Passive Voice for Historical Narration
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Bài nghe dùng nhiều câu bị động để trình bày lịch sử khách quan: 'rubber-soled shoes were first developed', 'The name was coined', 'canvas shoes had been adopted', 'Shoes that had been made to be worn were increasingly being collected'. Cấu trúc: be + past participle. Dùng bị động khi tác nhân không rõ hoặc không quan trọng bằng kết quả.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The text uses passive voice extensively for objective historical narration: 'rubber-soled shoes were first developed', 'The name was coined', 'had been adopted', 'were increasingly being collected'. Form: be + past participle. Use passive when the agent is unknown or less important than the result.

grammarNotes.title: Present Perfect and Past Perfect for Background Context
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Người kể chuyện dùng thì hiện tại hoàn thành để nối quá khứ với hiện tại: 'has become one of the most recognisable symbols', 'has been printed on the ankle patch ever since', 'has been criticised'. Quá khứ hoàn thành: 'canvas shoes had been adopted'. Công thức: have/has + V3 (hiện tại hoàn thành), had + V3 (quá khứ hoàn thành).
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The narrator uses present perfect to connect past events to the present: 'has become', 'has been printed ever since', 'has been criticised'. Past perfect: 'had been adopted'. Form: have/has + past participle (present perfect); had + past participle (past perfect).

translationVi:
Ít vật phẩm nào trong tủ quần áo hiện đại có hành trình kỳ lạ hơn chiếc giày thể thao. Điều bắt đầu như một giải pháp thực tế cho một vấn đề rất cụ thể đã trở thành một trong những biểu tượng dễ nhận biết nhất của văn hóa đại chúng trên toàn thế giới. Câu chuyện bắt đầu vào giữa thế kỷ mười chín, khi những đôi giày đế cao su đầu tiên được phát triển cho các vận động viên croquet và tennis cần giày êm, bám sàn trên sân cỏ. Tên gọi 'sneaker' thực ra được đặt ra vì đế cao su mềm giúp người đi có thể lén lút tiến đến gần người khác mà không tạo ra tiếng động. Các mẫu ban đầu nặng và khó chịu so với tiêu chuẩn ngày nay, nhưng chúng đại diện cho một sự thay đổi mạnh mẽ so với những đôi bốt da mà hầu hết người lao động mang mỗi ngày.

Vào đầu thế kỷ hai mươi, giày vải có đế cao su đã được các cầu thủ bóng rổ ở Mỹ chấp nhận. Converse All Star, ra mắt năm 1917, ban đầu được thiết kế như một đôi giày hiệu suất nghiêm túc. Nó được bảo trợ bởi cầu thủ Chuck Taylor, người có tên đã được in trên miếng dán cổ giày từ đó đến nay. Trong nhiều thập kỷ, giày thể thao được xem là đồ vật thuần túy thực dụng thuộc về sân đấu và đường chạy, không phải đường phố thành thị.

Điều đó bắt đầu thay đổi vào những năm 1970, khi chạy bộ trở nên thời thượng như một hoạt động giải trí và người dân thường bắt đầu mang giày thể thao ra ngoài phòng gym. Các công ty như Nike và Adidas, trước đây tập trung vào vận động viên chuyên nghiệp, nhận ra họ có thể bán trực tiếp cho thị trường đại chúng. Sự ra mắt công nghệ đệm khí Nike Air năm 1979 là một bước ngoặt, vì nó đưa giày thể thao vào lãnh địa của công nghệ hữu hình và đáng thèm muốn.

Vào những năm 1980, văn hóa hip-hop đã biến đổi giày thể thao thành tuyên ngôn về bản sắc. Các nhóm như Run-DMC nổi tiếng với việc mang giày Adidas shell-toes không buộc dây, và Adidas đáp lại bằng cách đề nghị họ một hợp đồng bảo trợ trị giá một triệu đô la — được cho là hợp đồng bảo trợ đầu tiên với một nhóm nhạc không phải vận động viên. Dòng Air Jordan, ra mắt hợp tác với Michael Jordan năm 1985, đã tạo ra thị trường giày thể thao hiện đại mà nhiều nhà phân tích mô tả. Những đôi giày được làm để đi ngày càng được sưu tập, trưng bày và trao đổi như tác phẩm nghệ thuật. Ngày nay, giày hiếm có thể bán đấu giá hàng trăm nghìn đô la.

Văn hóa giày thể thao đã bị chỉ trích vì khuyến khích chủ nghĩa tiêu dùng và vì điều kiện lao động bóc lột đôi khi thấy trong các nhà máy sản xuất. Nhưng nó cũng được khen ngợi vì dân chủ hóa thời trang — cho phép những người từ mọi hoàn cảnh kinh tế có cách thể hiện gu, bản sắc và sự gắn kết thông qua một vật phẩm không cần bộ vest hay túi xách thiết kế để báo hiệu địa vị.

questions.prompt: Why were rubber-soled shoes originally developed?
options: For croquet and tennis players who needed quiet, grippy shoes on grass
For factory workers who needed safe footwear
For long-distance runners on dirt roads
questions.explanation: The text says rubber-soled shoes were 'first developed for croquet and tennis players who needed quiet, grippy footwear on grass courts.'
questions.hint: The original users played a specific sport on a specific surface.

questions.prompt: Where does the word 'sneaker' come from?
options: From the brand name of an early manufacturer
From the soft rubber soles that let wearers sneak up on people silently
From a marketing campaign in the 1950s
From the word 'sneak', meaning to move quickly
questions.explanation: The text explains the name was 'coined because the soft rubber soles allowed wearers to sneak up on people without making any noise.'
questions.hint: The name refers to the quiet movement made possible by the material.

questions.prompt: What was the Converse All Star originally designed for?
questions.explanation: The text states it was 'originally designed as a serious performance shoe' for basketball.
questions.hint: Its original purpose was athletic, not fashionable.

questions.prompt: What changed sneakers from gym-only items to everyday wear in the 1970s?
options: A new type of rubber being invented
Jogging becoming a fashionable leisure activity
questions.explanation: The text says 'jogging became fashionable as a leisure activity and ordinary people started wearing trainers outside the gym.'
questions.hint: A popular fitness trend brought sneakers to the streets.

questions.prompt: What was significant about the Nike Air cushioning technology introduced in 1979?
options: It moved athletic shoes into the territory of visible, desirable technology
It made shoes much lighter than ever before
It was the first shoe endorsed by a celebrity
It allowed people to run faster
questions.explanation: The text says it 'moved athletic shoes into the territory of visible, desirable technology.'
questions.hint: The technology changed the cultural perception of the shoe.

questions.prompt: How did hip-hop culture change sneakers in the 1980s?
options: It moved them back to professional sports
It transformed them into a statement of identity
It made them less popular with young people
It led to the first mass-market sales campaign
questions.explanation: The text states 'hip-hop culture had transformed the sneaker into a statement of identity.'
questions.hint: Sneakers became about who you were, not just what you wore.

questions.prompt: What was notable about the Adidas deal with Run-DMC?
options: It was the first deal worth over ten million dollars
It was arranged by Michael Jordan
It was reportedly the first endorsement deal with a non-athlete act
It included a shoe designed specifically for hip-hop performances
questions.explanation: The text calls it 'reportedly the first endorsement deal of its kind with a non-athlete act.'
questions.hint: The novelty was in who received the deal, not just the money.

questions.prompt: When was the Air Jordan line launched?
questions.explanation: The text states the Air Jordan line was 'launched in collaboration with Michael Jordan in 1985.'
questions.hint: Look for the year mentioned alongside Michael Jordan.

questions.prompt: How has sneaker culture been criticised?
options: For encouraging consumerism and for exploitative labour conditions in factories
For making people less interested in professional sports
For reducing the quality of athletic footwear
For being too expensive for working-class consumers
questions.explanation: The text says it 'has been criticised for encouraging consumerism and for the exploitative labour conditions sometimes found in the factories.'

questions.prompt: How has sneaker culture been praised?
options: For reducing the cost of professional sports equipment
For democratising fashion and allowing people of all backgrounds to express identity
For creating millions of factory jobs worldwide
For encouraging children to exercise more
questions.explanation: The text praises it 'for democratising fashion — giving people from all economic backgrounds a way to express taste, identity, and belonging.'
questions.hint: The praise is about access and expression.

questions.prompt: What comparison does the narrator use to describe the status of rare sneakers today?
options: They are compared to vintage cars
They are described as investments similar to gold
They are collected, displayed, and traded like art
They are seen as museum pieces
questions.explanation: The text says rare sneakers were 'increasingly being collected, displayed, and traded like art.'
questions.hint: The comparison is to a creative field.

questions.prompt: According to the text, what can a sneaker do that a suit or designer handbag also does?
options: Indicate wealth through its price tag
questions.explanation: The text says sneakers allow people to 'signal status' without a suit or designer handbag.
questions.hint: The sneaker is placed in the same category as traditional luxury markers.

cloze.template:
What started as a practical solution to a very specific problem has become one of the most recognisable symbols of popular culture around the world. The story begins in the mid-nineteenth century, when {{b1}}-soled shoes were first developed for croquet and tennis players who needed quiet, grippy footwear on grass courts. The name 'sneaker' was actually {{b2}} because the soft rubber soles allowed wearers to sneak up on people without making any noise. Early models were heavy and uncomfortable by today's standards, but they represented a radical {{b3}} from the leather boots that most working people wore every day.

By the early twentieth century, canvas shoes with rubber soles had been {{b4}} by basketball players in the United States. The Converse All Star was originally designed as a serious performance shoe. It was endorsed by the player Chuck Taylor, whose name has been printed on the ankle patch ever {{b5}}.

That began to change in the 1970s, when jogging became fashionable as a {{b6}} activity and ordinary people started wearing trainers outside the gym. The introduction of the Nike Air {{b7}} technology in 1979 was a turning point, because it moved athletic shoes into the territory of visible, desirable technology.

By the 1980s, hip-hop culture had transformed the sneaker into a statement of {{b8}}. The Air Jordan line, launched in collaboration with Michael Jordan in 1985, created what many analysts have described as the modern sneaker market.

Sneaker culture has been {{b9}} for encouraging consumerism and for the exploitative labour conditions sometimes found in the factories that produce these shoes. But it has also been praised for {{b10}} fashion — giving people from all economic backgrounds a way to express taste, identity, and belonging.

blanks.explanation: 'Rubber-soled shoes' is the exact phrase used — rubber is the material that made sneakers distinctive.
'Coined' specifically means a new word or term was created — it collocates with naming words or phrases.
'Radical departure' is the exact fixed phrase meaning a significant change in direction.
'Adopted' is used here in the passive — meaning taken up and regularly used, which is the standard collocation.
'Ever since' is a fixed phrase meaning 'from that time until now'.
'Leisure activity' is the exact phrase — distinguishing recreational jogging from professional sport.
'Air cushioning technology' is the exact name used for Nike's innovation.
'Statement of identity' is the exact phrase — sneakers became about who you are, not just what you wear.
'Criticised' is the correct word — the text balances criticism and praise, both using the passive.
'Democratising fashion' is the exact phrase — meaning making fashion accessible to all classes.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The text describes sneakers as both a symbol of harmful consumerism and a tool for democratising fashion. Do you think an object can genuinely do both at the same time, or does one cancel the other out? Give reasons using examples from the text or your own experience.
